edge: Chain-of-Thought reasoning has emerged as a promising approach for enhancing the performance of large language models on complex reasoning tasks.
A series of studies attempt to explain the mechanisms underlying Chain-of-Thought reasoning to deepen the understanding of its efficacy.
A lack of quantitative metrics to assess CoT capabilities is one of the major challenges in existing research.
The dearth of guidance on optimizing CoT performance is a major challenge addressed by the proposed reasoning boundary framework.
The novel reasoning boundary framework (RBF) addresses the lack of quantification in CoT by defining a reasoning boundary (RB) to quantify the upper-bound of CoT.
The reasoning boundary framework addresses the lack of optimization in CoT by proposing categories of RBs and optimizing them for CoT improvement.
The proposed categories of RBs are optimized with combination laws focused on RB promotion and reasoning path optimization for CoT improvement.
The study validates the proposed framework and explains the effectiveness of 10 CoT strategies, guiding optimization strategies for reasoning in LLMs.
LLMs can benefit from optimization strategies for reasoning, leading to enhanced performance in various tasks.
GPT and PaLM are advanced LLMs with emergent capabilities, demonstrating potential advancements in natural language processing.
PaLM and LlaMa series are part of the advanced LLMs showcasing emergent capabilities like Chain-of-Thought for step-by-step reasoning.
Chain-of-Thought methodology enables models to base decisions on logical rationale, enhancing prediction accuracy through step-by-step reasoning.
Recent research in the literature has focused on investigating the mechanism of Chain-of-Thought to enhance understanding of its operational nature.

The reasoning boundary framework (RBF) is introduced to optimize the boundaries of current LLMs, motivated by enhancing model capabilities.
A new concept named reasoning boundary (RB) is proposed to quantify the upper-bound on task-specific reasoning complexity within a model to address the quantification challenge.
The combination law of RBs is presented to generalize the RB for quantification in more real and complex scenarios.

Three reasoning boundary intervals are proposed and analyzed to guide optimization through improved RB and optimized reasoning paths based on the combination law to address the CoT optimization challenge.
The optimization based on reasoning boundary intervals achieves state-of-the-art performance in the proposed benchmark.
Based on the lack of CoT optimization, three categories of RBs are defined to address this issue.
The combination law and the nature of RBs are essential concepts in the context, where the understanding of RBs is crucial for improving CoT strategies through reasoning path optimization.
RB promotion is a strategy used to improve CoT strategies, indicating a direct relationship between CoT strategies and RB promotion.
CoT strategies can be enhanced through reasoning path optimization, highlighting the link between these two concepts in the context.
The framework is validated on 27 models and 5 CoT tasks, demonstrating the application and rationality of the framework in various scenarios.
Optimal performance in CoT strategies is explained through two optimization perspectives, illustrating the rationale behind achieving state-of-the-art performance.
The concept of a minimum acceptable reasoning path (MARP) aims to prompt achieving state-of-the-art performance in CoT tasks, emphasizing the importance of efficient reasoning paths.

Reasoning Boundary (RB) is introduced as a concept to quantify the capacity for complex reasoning in LLMs, providing a framework for evaluating the ease of handling a specific reasoning process within the models.
RB reflects the limit at which a model's accuracy significantly degrades, indicating the critical role RB plays in determining the accuracy of the models.
